claim: 2026 will see a major shift from subsidized request-based API pricing to consumption-based token pricing, creating per-person AI budgeting problems that have no historical precedent.
actionable: Begin planning AI spend governance now: establish decision frameworks for how much token budget each role gets, similar to department budgeting but at individual IC level.
source_title: The best engineers don't write the most code. They delete the most code. — Stay Sassy

claim: Individual AI engineering costs could easily reach $50k-$100k+ per year per person (with some reaching $2.5M/year for heavily AI-augmented roles), upending traditional engineering economics.
actionable: Model your AI infrastructure costs as person-level line items in budgets, not just departmental; prepare for scenarios where token spend rivals or exceeds salary.
source_title: The best engineers don't write the most code. They delete the most code. — Stay Sassy

claim: Build vs. buy calculus is shifting: AI-assisted building is now so cheap that many internal tools become economically competitive vs. external SaaS, but maintenance complexity remains underestimated.
actionable: For build vs. buy decisions: list required features, estimate maintenance burden (schema changes, vendor lock-in, uptime SLAs), then decide—don't be swayed by cheap build-time alone.
source_title: The best engineers don't write the most code. They delete the most code. — Stay Sassy

claim: Most mature SaaS products are far more complex than they appear; a good heuristic is: if you can't replace it with a spreadsheet MVP, it's probably too hard to build internally.
actionable: Before building, prototype with Google Sheets; if the sheets MVP doesn't capture 80% of value, the full product is more complex than it looks—reconsider building.
source_title: The best engineers don't write the most code. They delete the most code. — Stay Sassy

claim: Two separate automation opportunities: task automation (delegation) vs. decision automation (strategic judgment); most executives focus on task automation but strategic decisions are lower-hanging fruit.
actionable: Map your executives' decisions: ~70% are standard/delegatable; document decision trees, then automate those with AI; focus humans on the 30% that truly need judgment.
source_title: The best engineers doesn't write the most code. They delete the most code. — Stay Sassy

claim: Executive augmentation with AI is neglected compared to IC-level automation, yet executives are often bottlenecks; automating standard decisions frees them for unique problems.
actionable: As an executive, identify your top 5 recurring decisions, write decision criteria (not AI-prompts—actual rules), then encode them; redirect time to non-standard problems.
source_title: The best engineers don't write the most code. They delete the most code. — Stay Sassy

claim: Code review becomes MORE critical with AI coding tools, not less; fatigue from managing many agents makes high-stakes PR review risky—teams must own code quality collectively.
actionable: If using AI coding tools, enforce stricter PR review protocols: no single reviewer should review critical code when fatigued; rotate reviewers, require team sign-off on risky changes.
source_title: The best engineers don't write the most code. They delete the most code. — Stay Sassy

claim: Managing many AI agents simultaneously (10x→100x productivity narrative) is cognitively exhausting; reviewing complex PRs after that exhaustion creates production risk.
actionable: If your review load is high from AI tools, schedule high-complexity reviews early in the day; consider rotating review duties to prevent fatigue-induced bugs.
source_title: The best engineers don't write the most code. They delete the most code. — Stay Sassy

claim: Reliable production systems require culture where the team (not individuals) owns safety: no single low-performer or new hire can take down prod without multiple failing checks.
actionable: Establish team code-review culture: make it normal for peers to review each other's work; penalize shipping without review; treat new hires as team responsibility, not lone risks.
source_title: The best engineers don't write the most code. They delete the most code. — Stay Sassy

claim: Most leadership decisions are knowable via precedent; the bottleneck is not lack of information but willpower to execute known-good decisions.
actionable: When facing a leadership decision, ask 'have others solved this?' If yes, apply their solution; resist the temptation to re-solve as a differentiator.
source_title: The best engineers don't write the most code. They delete the most code. — Stay Sassy

claim: AI systems that automate decision-making (without removing human judgment) should be transparent about their decision criteria; humans should still be able to override.
actionable: When building AI decision tools, log the reasoning; never hide decision criteria; always allow human override and veto for edge cases.
source_title: The best engineers don't write the most code. They delete the most code. — Stay Sassy

claim: Series A/B lessons remain durable across technology shifts; AI companies face the same scaling challenges (hiring, culture, product-market fit) as pre-AI startups.
actionable: Don't assume AI startups need entirely new playbooks; apply proven Series A frameworks: focus on team, unit economics, and defensibility—the tools change, not the fundamentals.
source_title: The best engineers don't write the most code. They delete the most code. — Stay Sassy

claim: Managing humans remains the hardest and most irreplaceable leadership skill; AI cannot replicate trust-building, emotional support, and judgment calls about people.
actionable: Invest heavily in your management skills and team relationships; this is where AI cannot help and where the best leaders will differentiate.
source_title: The best engineers don't write the most code. They delete the most code. — Stay Sassy

claim: The culture shift from 'shipping speed at all costs' to 'shipping speed with team safety' is difficult but necessary; Amazon's incident shows scale doesn't exempt companies.
actionable: Audit your team's code review culture: if PRs are being merged without review to 'move fast,' you're taking on tail risk; institute mandatory review for production code.
source_title: The best engineers don't write the most code. They delete the most code. — Stay Sassy

claim: Internal tool UI customization should be user-modifiable; standardized UIs constrain productivity and hide complexity under 'design consistency.'
actionable: For internal tools, build APIs first, then UI; let users customize UI without touching backend logic or data models.
source_title: The best engineers don't write the most code. They delete the most code. — Stay Sassy
